# R2.04 · Cross-shard consistency

> Detect and avoid torn writes across instances using version tokens and commit markers when atomic multi-key
> operations are not possible. EchoMQ's real answer is PREVENTION by colocation — a multi-key Lua EVAL requires every
> key on one slot, so the braced `{q}` hashtag (`EchoMQ.Keyspace.queue_key` → `emq:{q}:*`) lands a queue's keys on one
> slot and `@claim` runs atomically (a cross-slot EVAL raises `CROSSSLOT`); `attempts` (`HINCRBY`) is the monotone
> version token a stale writer fails against (`EMQSTALE`). These detection patterns are the fallback when keys
> genuinely cannot share a slot. Served at /redis-patterns/coordination/cross-shard-consistency.

## Pages

- [Hub](/redis-patterns/coordination/cross-shard-consistency): the torn write, the detection patterns, and EchoMQ's prevention by colocation.
- [Torn writes](/redis-patterns/coordination/cross-shard-consistency/torn-writes): why a multi-key write off one slot can tear, and why echo_mq cannot tear — one slot + one Lua EVAL is atomic.
- [Version tokens](/redis-patterns/coordination/cross-shard-consistency/version-tokens): ordering — `attempts` (monotone via `HINCRBY`) as the applied version token; a stale writer is caught by token mismatch (`EMQSTALE`); `{emq}:version` → `echomq:2.0.0` is the protocol fence.
- [Commit markers](/redis-patterns/coordination/cross-shard-consistency/commit-markers): the generic two-phase marker; echo_mq needs none because the single Lua EVAL over co-located keys is the serialization (`@complete`).

## Up

- [R2 · Coordination](/redis-patterns/coordination) · [Course home](/redis-patterns)
